chapters.title: How to make a baby à la française
chapters.text:
All families are dysfunctional in their own beautiful ways. But French families know how to hide it well. Parisian mothers always have a diaper and their favorite lipstick in their purses. What seems like a betrayal of parents' solidarity worldwide, the rules of French parenting are simple yet complex at first sight.

A happy family means the well-being of all its members, not just a newborn raptor. Adult time has never been an excuse for French parents but an opportunity to recharge and appreciate each other's efforts. As for the children, the French air seems to sedate them better than breast milk. Jokes aside, it's the experience of generations of French parents with ridiculously logical views on feeding, lulling, and behaving. The fundamental rule is to trust your child as you trust yourself and book that vacation.

Some people righteously see French parents as overly strict. It is an American view on parenting, as overindulgence is a severe problem on the other side of the Atlantic. What French parents do have is boundaries. Furthermore, they teach their kids to have them, too. These are not just self-worth and confidence but also being mannerly and considerate. In these words, we simply guess the natural Parisian.

Parenting knowledge comes from values distilled in us as children.

As an Anglophone in Paris, Pamela Druckerman struggled to become a real Parisian mother. Used to American leniency and snacking as a pacifier, she did not expect to raise her children Francophone. But her mind drastically changed when she saw how calm and well-behaved French kids are. Eventually, she adopted the new mindset and raised three rather French children.

chapters.title: Relax and take a deep breath
chapters.text:
Before we take a trip down parenthood, there's one critical stop: romance. Every couple is different, although some general rules exist for such an engagement — especially an international one. For example, French and Americans may struggle to find common ground because of their cultural differences. So, before the baby enters this world, future parents must create a balanced emotional climate. It also means they must take it easy long before the due date.

The routine always follows the romance, so we must learn to deal with our partners' before it's too late. Moving in together requires space for two. Bad habits can be deal breakers. However, adjusting them to the shared routine inspires understanding. The other thing is the mindset.

Choosing yourself is a necessary thing in relationships.

All nationalities have quirks, but Americans and French compete in opposites. Americans are chronically overwhelmed and neurotic, while the French seem to be always calm and unbothered, whether in a relationship or pregnancy. This is why they can't understand each other. A husband who buys himself a croissant in the middle of his wife's labor is the quintessence of most French husbands. It doesn't mean he's ignorant. He just stays calm and doesn't dramatize. French soon-to-be mothers are the same. That's why epidurals are so popular in France.

When pregnant, American women hope to absorb any possible information about changes in nutrition, lifestyle, and sex life after the two lines. With so many restrictions from doctors, books, and blogs, it's easy to develop panic attacks.

French women are more laid-back. There's a certain beauty in how calm and relaxed they are when expecting. A major difference between American and French mothers-to-be is caring about themselves. Parisian women approach parenting differently, with a more relaxed attitude towards food choices, routine changes, and abstinence — or lack thereof — even before giving birth.

Smoking and drinking are some of the things pregnant women should never do. American women stand by this restriction. But in France, a glass of wine or a cigarette while childbearing is not a forbidden fruit. It does not mean women there don't care about their future children. The flip side of this behavior is staying an authentic personality. It is also why pregnancy magazines in France recommend sex positions and women never overeat to remain thin. In other words, a French woman doesn't confine herself.

chapters.title: Delaying Delight: The French Art of Waiting for Sweets
chapters.text:
Caprices and children seem to go hand in hand, and no one considers it a problem except for the French. Just as Parisian mothers value their self-time, many French parents never sacrifice their sleep or schedule for a whiny child. As self-centered as it may sound, it is for the child's future.

A child's personality is not a result of nature's lottery. Instead, it comes from parents' observance. Some mothers with hard-to-sleep toddlers never suspect an issue. According to studies, almost half of American infants wake up at night. Still, only a small number of parents consider it problematic. And while it is a signal to act differently, the changes must be at the root. And it is not only about sleep but overall upbringing.

Children are clever and teachable, yet parents often overlook it. The second a toddler cries in their crib, the panicked mother rushes to feed or change. In her mind, she's a lousy parent if she doesn't respond. But French mothers rarely feel guilty. Instead, they examine their child.

Research on infants found that babies often move and murmur in their sleep, so a parent can mistakenly wake them up, disrupting healthy sleep development. Babies are capable of bridging their sleeping cycles, just like adults. Sometimes, it takes a tear or two. But in the end, they learn to be by themselves instead of making their mother's cradling a habit. Being patient and observing a child's sleep saves time for the other business. However, the first four months are critical. After that, their sleeping habits become solid.

Acknowledging a child means providing them with the structure for better socialization.

Speaking of patience, French children are miraculously calm. First of all, they sleep well, avoiding irritability or impulsive actions. And second, their parents taught them to wait. It starts with regular meals at age four months and continues with something like interrupting.

French parents are strict with integrating their offspring into the family's dynamic. They achieve this by having them wait for short periods, usually just a few minutes, before giving them what they want, whether it's a piece of cake or their parent’s attention. Parents trust their child's adequacy when explaining why they can't get what they crave. Simultaneously, they refocus kids' attention on other things. Children then learn self-control and respect for others' needs and can entertain themselves without parents.

chapters.title: There’s no need to rush nature
chapters.text:
French parents may seem too strict with their children. But this impression appears because we don’t see the other side of their parenting. Like all other mothers and fathers, people in France care about their offspring's happiness. So, while they're building a frame of actions on the one hand, they provide freedom within it on the other.

The philosophy of French parenting stems from the teachings of Jean-Jacque Rousseau, who emphasized the importance of “awakening.” On one side, he encourages strict limits and authority. When the child grows used to many pleasures, their desires grow. And when they don't receive satisfaction, tantrums are inevitable. Suppose the parent doesn't reaffirm the superiority of their opinion as a more experienced adult. In that case, the child will ignore others' boundaries. It equals failed socialization.

Speaking of socialization, the French pay special attention to it. Rousseau encouraged letting children roam and explore. Doing so makes them open to new experiences in the chaotic world. The thinker also believed parents' role was to create a safe, limited space with rules and duties that helped children adapt to society. This is where French strictness originates from.

On the other hand, they value every little experience their offspring has. Enrolling their children in various classes like swimming, music, and arts is considered essential by many parents in France, or at least in Paris. However, the reasons for doing it differ between the French and the American perspectives.

In the US, families take their children to classes to speed up their development often at the expense of kids' interests. A wunderkind becomes a “currency” to show parents' achievements. Bragging about a child's fast learning or being ahead of their peers became a bad habit. However, the endpoint of such behavior is unclear.

Caring for a child's mentality bears more fruits in the future than showcasing their achievements.

In France, parents are more philosophical about their kids' upbringing. They don't push one developmental stage ahead of another. They don't see the point. At every age, a baby learns precisely what they need to strive as a social being. Instead of achieving unnecessary milestones ahead of time, children are allowed to be children. They have permission to wonder, be surprised, experiment, and make conclusions. In other words, they develop slowly and naturally.

chapters.title: Orbiting around your child is not OK
chapters.text:
One of the reasons why French kids are “entitled” to fill themselves with experiences is their mothers' detachment. Unlike American mums, French mums never exclude themselves from the community, which includes work, friends, and dates. They return to work as fast as possible to avoid slipping off Paris's social bandwagon. And they have all rational reasons to do so.

A stable income has one significant benefit 一 independence. While the problem of female economic dependence on husbands is still severe, there is also a chance of divorce. In both cases, a woman has to provide for her children and herself. As for the social component, Druckerman mentioned that French people find non-working mothers “boring.” Giving these women the cold shoulder, they ignore the “maternity pass.” The new mother must stay active and engage in self-development and the community's life.

French women worry about the quality of their lives and can't afford for kids to overtake it. Unlike the American parenting tradition, they don't spend every minute with their offspring, whether at a playground, a birthday party, or home.

We bring children into the world but avoid letting them live in it.

At the same time, French mums are not terse or egotistical. Instead, they're practical. Their self-time has benefits for them and the child. They are not weary and discouraged after losing social presence, while kids discover more people and environment than just home.

Some American mothers view their children as projects, enrolling them in numerous classes to highlight their efforts as parents. French mothers can cancel a class if it doesn't fit their schedule unless their child enjoys it. Classes are more entertaining than skill-building in their vision. There is no sense of competition with other mums over whose hidden guilt is stronger.

The perfect mother doesn't exist... ~ Pamela Druckerman

Pamela Druckerman

Speaking of guilt, French mothers also receive social criticism, especially regarding their postpartum weight. In France, losing baby fat is an unspoken rule. The habit most Parisian females adopt subconsciously is paying attention to what and how much they eat.

They don't stick to a diet but simply regulate their nutrition when gaining weight. Ultimately, this tradition is relatively healthy but not without a grain of social expectations.

chapters.title: Politeness is a French institution
chapters.text:
There's no more doubt the strictest Parisian mother does it concerning their child's well-being. After all, France's education institute has followed the rules installed in its society for decades. The first years of a kid's life are formative. Early education in France pays significant attention to teaching kids basic social skills.

The crèche, a French nursery, is the first step on the education path. Most Parisian parents squeeze into enrollment lists to fit their children. New mothers wait as short as three months postpartum to return to the workforce. Enrolling their one- or two-year-olds in a crèche helps them work without worrying about the baby's wellness. Despite being uneasy due to separation, French mothers believe that not putting their kids in crèche robs them of essential experiences.

Family is a close circle with limited experience. Society provides more amortization into upbringing.

Politeness is one of the fundamental pillars of French society. Integrating a child into this system takes parents and a school. There are four phrases a French child has to know: bonjour (hello), merci (thank you), s'il vous plaît (please), and au revoir (goodbye). In France, exchanging greetings has a philosophical and cultural meaning. The first thing kids learn is bonjour. By greeting people, we give them a share of our attention, recognizing their presence. In other words, we respect them. This belief applies to French kids, too.

Furthermore, they integrate better into society, being seen as a person despite their young age. This way, children realize their and others' right to take space.

Speaking of the latter, the idea of preschool, or màternelle, in France focuses on awareness, respect, and collaboration. Children learn to be social, organize their opinions, and speak out. Considering how difficult socialization can be for some adults, encouraging this skill in kids is crucial for their self-image and status.

Following instructions is another skill màternelle focuses on. In a group of children with one teacher, a kid grasps the concepts of an individual and a group. At the same time, they learn to contribute, playing by the rules. All in all, màternelle resembles a miniature society where kids can start their integration with other peers.

Did you know? Research by the Center for the Developing Adolescent shows that warm, respectful, and supportive parenting leads to adolescents with lower risks of anxiety, depression, and substance abuse, and promotes maturity, resilience, and academic success.

chapters.title: Marriage is not a love triangle
chapters.text:
In marriage, there always comes a moment when both must prioritize the child's upbringing or romance. The problem is gnawing at most families across the Atlantic. But in France, this issue has yet to spike, shockingly. The main reason is building boundaries between children and their personal lives.

Until now, we've witnessed the rules French children must follow during the first years of their lives, among which respect for others' space is primary. Their parents grew up with the same framework, which explains the importance of privacy in marriage.

French people are famous romantics. Couples with children see romance as crucial, especially after birth. Sex and alone time are fundamental for family strength, including children. Stress, accumulated during the first months after the baby is born, negatively affects all sides. When parents prioritize their life as a couple and maintain a healthy relationship, they tend to argue or snap at their children less frequently. This approach emphasizes the importance of the love union over children, resulting in stronger French marriages.

While parents love and cherish their children, they never feel guilty for scheduling a time for two. French people don't schedule dates like a visit to the doctor. They don't view romance as a deviation from regular life because this is their regular life.

Giving up on intimacy creates a psychological imbalance and ruins relationships.

On the other hand, the speed of gender equality in France is significantly lower than in the US. In France, women perform 89% of household work, whereas in America, only 31%. Yet, French women rarely complain about it for three reasons:

• The state funds child care, health services, and college funds (as well as perineal reeducation)
• Women have more career opportunities, especially after giving birth, so they don't get stuck at home
• Wives have fewer expectations of their husbands regarding house chores

Still, wives and mothers in Paris don't do everything themselves. American women are more likely to do everything themselves to avoid excessive explanation. However, in France, females turn some control over to nannies or parents when it comes to children. Then, they can spend time with their husbands, take themselves on a date, or develop professionally.

There's this fundamental assumption that every human being has desire. It never disappears for very long. If it does it means you're depressed and you need to be treated. ~ Marie–Anne Suizzo

Pamela Druckerman

chapters.title: Here comes the airplane
chapters.text:
Everyone has a specific food to call an archenemy. Olives, fish, parsley, or a spoonful of marmite can cause a dramatic gag reflex. When we think about the time we grew our dislike, childhood seems to be the first thought. It's no wonder as our parents weren't French.

In France, parents are responsible for refining their kids' tastes. And just like teaching kids patience and respect, French parents do the same with eating. For a better understanding of taste priorities between the two countries, French restaurants don't have a kids' menu, unlike American ones. There, children must try “adult” meals as they are used to various tastes already.

French children have four mealtimes a day: breakfast, lunch, goûter (an afternoon snack), and dinner. Parents, especially mothers, train them to wait from one meal to another. That's why seeing a French kid snacking on a chocolate bar is a rarity.

Child obesity in France is relatively low compared to other European countries. The government also promotes healthy, moderate eating in all age groups. The “five fruits and vegetables a day” campaign has succeeded remarkably.

The main secret of raising a little cheese connoisseur is starting with rich flavors straightaway. The most popular options are pureed vegetables like spinach, green beans, zucchini, or carrots.

In the French mindset, there are no offputting tastes or smells. Suppose the baby doesn't like broccoli the first time. Then, parents experiment with its textures and cooking methods until the child accepts it. Adjusting, not enforcing, is critical. Textural and visual diversity keeps the child's attention and helps maintain a healthy diet. The latter means giving kids snacks and chocolate during goûter, so they don't crave them outside the mealtimes.

Conversations are a critical part of mealtime. It satiates along with food.

Another secret is discussing food with a baby. When the child receives a piece of carrot or a grape, parents should ask:

• When you bite it, what sound do you hear?
• How do you feel in your mouth?
• What do you think of the smell?

Asking open-ended questions helps develop a child's associations and intelligence.

The final secret is to keep dinners to a maximum of thirty minutes. French children are encouraged to taste every ingredient and don't need to finish their meals. This habit helps to cultivate a healthy relationship with food.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Parenthood is one of the most challenging roles an adult can have. On the other hand, it is also not taken so seriously. Raising children can bring about significant stress, which young parents often underestimate. As a result, they're usually unprepared before the inevitable hardships. However, in France, the subject of parenthood has a different twist to it.

French parents never forget that they're individuals, too. Complete obedience to a baby exhausts most young parents. In Paris, however, children participate in society from a very young age. But to prepare their offspring to be active individuals, it takes knowledge. On the other hand, thoughtful rearing will have positive effects in the future.

A thriving family comes from a balanced relationship between spouses. Romance and intimacy never disappear from French marriages, even during pregnancy.

Parisians have a generational intuition to help them raise children. When the baby arrives, they start teaching them to have eight hours of sleep. Trusting babies to regulate their sleep can result in parents getting better sleep.

The same goes for politeness. Although parents are in charge of good habits, this responsibility comes from the expectations they have for children. From a young age, kids participate in adult rituals like greetings and having a rich palette. It is also why no French parent glues themselves to their child. Combining self-value and trust in their children makes Parisians some of the calmest and luckiest parents.

Try this


• Don't use baby talk with your child. Practice proper vocabulary as soon as possible.
• Separate family and adult time. Children are capable of entertaining themselves. It reduces dependency on others.
• Build manners from an early age, as it becomes harder later. Children will also navigate themselves better.

Read it till the end?
